title: Piping basketweave
subtitle: Interlaced ribbed buttercream pattern on cake sides
excerpt: A classic cake-decorating technique using a flat serrated piping tip to create horizontal and vertical ribbed stripes that interlock into a woven basket pattern. Requires a turntable and steady, consistent pressure.
sourceNotes: Basketweave piping is a standard formal cake-decorating technique documented in professional patisserie texts from the early twentieth century. It is one of the original trained piping patterns and appears in British confectionery guides from the 1930s. The technique requires a flat basketweave tip (sometimes called a ribbon tip) rather than a star or round nozzle.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Apply basketweave to a crumb-coated, chilled cake for best results. The finished cake can be refrigerated overnight.

content.text: Basketweave piping works on a simple alternating grid: a vertical stripe, then horizontal stripes crossing over it at regular intervals, then another vertical stripe offset to cover the gaps. The pattern repeats around the cake. The flat serrated (basketweave) tip gives the ribbed texture that reads as woven. The cake should already be crumb-coated so the surface is stable.

Consistent pressure matters more than speed. Fill the piping bag no more than half full and keep a steady, even squeeze throughout each stroke. An erratic squeeze shows in the width of each stripe. Practice a row on baking paper before moving to the cake.

Fill a piping bag fitted with a flat basketweave nozzle (ridged side facing outward) with buttercream. Hold the bag at a 45° angle to the cake side. Pipe one vertical stripe from the top to the base of the cake. This stripe runs the full height and is slightly wider than the nozzle tip.

Starting at the top of the cake, pipe a horizontal stripe across the vertical stripe, going from left to right. The stripe should be the width of the nozzle tip. Leave a gap equal to one nozzle width below it, then pipe another horizontal stripe. Continue down the full height of the cake, alternating stripes and gaps. Each horizontal stripe starts at the left edge of the vertical stripe and extends one nozzle width beyond it to the right.
Add the second vertical, then repeat

Pipe a second vertical stripe immediately to the right of the horizontal stripe ends. This stripe covers the right ends of the horizontal stripes. Now pipe a second set of horizontal stripes, but offset by one nozzle width: start in the gaps left by the first set. Each new horizontal stripe starts at the left edge of the first vertical stripe and ends at the right edge of the second vertical stripe. Continue the pattern around the cake, rotating the turntable as you work.
